title: Top Banana

jokes.text: The narrator teases the banana stand fire as 'a delicious whodunit'
jokes.setup: Serious news-broadcast tone describing a landmark burning down
jokes.punchline: 'A delicious whodunit' — 'delicious' is both a literal descriptor of frozen bananas and a pun on the murder-mystery genre
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan 'delicious whodunit' is a tight, elegant pun that does double duty: it's funny on first watch and gains extra appreciation on rewatch when you know the whole plot. The economy is exceptional — two words carry a layered joke. Slightly below an 8 because it's a single-word pun without the structural complexity of the show's best jokes.

jokes.text: George Sr. in prison: 'There's always money in the banana stand' while lovingly eating an ice cream sandwich and trying to share it
jokes.setup: Michael urgently needs money and financial records; the SEC has locked everything down
jokes.punchline: George Sr. responds with cryptic but literal advice ('there's always money in the banana stand') while being completely distracted by an ice cream sandwich, offering Michael a bite mid-crisis
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's central joke engine. George Sr. is simultaneously giving Michael the exact answer he needs AND withholding it through absurd distraction and oblique phrasing. 'There's always money in the banana stand' is both the literal truth ($250k in the walls) and sounds like folksy nonsense advice. The ice cream sandwich bit perfectly captures George Sr.'s self-absorbed prison-as-vacation delusion. High character integration — only George Sr. could deliver cryptic financial wisdom while aggressively offering dessert bites. Earns rewatch bonus because its full meaning only lands at episode's end.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I haven't had a vacation in years. This is my vacation.' / 'I'm doing the time of my life!'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if George Sr. was taking vacations on the company jet, implying he's hiding something
jokes.punchline: George Sr. sincerely reframes federal prison as his vacation, culminating in 'I'm doing the time of my life!' — a pun on 'having the time of my life'
jokes.explanation: A two-part joke that escalates beautifully. First the absurdist reframe (prison = vacation, complete with 'exercising' and 'sleeping well'), then the punchline wordplay: 'doing the time' means both serving a prison sentence AND 'having the time of my life.' Excellent economy and character work — George Sr.'s complete denial of his situation is a defining character trait delivered with perfect confidence. Quotability is high; 'I'm doing the time of my life!' is a standout line.

jokes.text: George Sr.'s prison roommate T-Bone is introduced as 'a flamer'
jokes.setup: Michael meets T-Bone, George Sr.'s prison roommate, and George Sr. casually describes him as 'a flamer'
jokes.punchline: Later revealed T-Bone is literally an arsonist — 'a flamer' = someone who starts fires — subverting the obvious double entendre reading
jokes.explanation: Classic Arrested Development layered wordplay. 'Flamer' lands with one meaning on first watch (the crude slang), then is retroactively redefined as literal (arsonist). The show gets comedy from both readings simultaneously, and the payoff at 11:29 ('definitely the work of a flamer') makes this even richer in retrospect. High originality because the misdirection is earned through plot, not just cheap wordplay. The rewatch bonus is massive — knowing T-Bone burned the storage unit makes the introduction scene land completely differently.

jokes.text: Michael, after meeting T-Bone leaving prison: 'Well, I guess every vacation's gotta come to an end.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has just called prison his 'vacation'; T-Bone is checking out (being released)
jokes.punchline: Michael deadpans the vacation metaphor back sincerely, treating prison release as an ordinary end-of-holiday moment
jokes.explanation: Efficient callback joke that uses Michael's characteristic dry wit. By playing along with George Sr.'s delusional framing, Michael's straight-man persona gets a rare wry moment. Works well because it doesn't over-explain — Michael just lets the absurdity breathe.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Because he's a flamer?' after Michael says he won't hire T-Bone
jokes.setup: Michael says he won't give T-Bone a job because they have no money
jokes.punchline: George Sr. assumes (or pretends to assume) it's discrimination — 'because he's a flamer?' — while Michael has to admit he did tell George Sr. that 'in confidence'
jokes.explanation: The flamer joke deepens — now we see that Michael himself used the word (presumably in a different sense than George Sr. intended), and the tables are turned on him. Michael's 'I told you that in confidence' is a great awkward moment that rewards close attention. Rewatch bonus because knowing T-Bone is an arsonist makes George Sr.'s apparent 'mistake' possibly completely sincere.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George Michael was having a hard time adjusting to his new one' — cut to cramped van with 'ass to ankles' crowding
jokes.setup: Narrator says George Michael is 'adjusting to his new roommate' — implying a difficult domestic situation
jokes.punchline: The new roommate is his cousin Maeby in the family van — Tobias shouts 'we are just about ass to ankles back here' — the absurd cramped living situation is the visual reveal
jokes.explanation: 'Ass to ankles' is a memorably crude phrase delivered with cheerful practicality by Tobias. The narrator's straight setup followed by the visual absurdity of the whole family crammed in a van works well. The phrase itself is quotable and weird enough to stick.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Whoa, bumpy road ahead.' — presumably while driving the cramped van with his stepdaughter on his lap
jokes.setup: The family is crammed ass-to-ankles in the van; Tobias has told Maeby to 'hop on your cousin's lap'
jokes.punchline: Tobias cheerfully warns of a bumpy road in a context that makes the statement deeply uncomfortable given the seating arrangement
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias obliviousness — he's completely unaware of the uncomfortable subtext his words create. The economy is excellent (four words do a lot of cringe work), though the joke leans on the audience making the connection themselves. Tobias's characteristic inability to read a room is well-integrated here.

jokes.text: George Michael asks for more hours at the banana stand; Michael flashes back to his own childhood there
jokes.setup: George Michael says he wants to work more hours at the banana stand; Michael is touched and nostalgic
jokes.punchline: Michael's nostalgia is framed as sweet, but the audience knows George Michael's real motive is to escape the cramped van — the gap between Michael's warmth and George Michael's ulterior motive is the joke
jokes.explanation: A quiet structural irony — Michael's heartwarming moment is undercut by the audience's knowledge of George Michael's real motivation. Not a huge laugh but a well-constructed ironic beat that rewards attentive viewers. The comedy is subtle and mostly situational.

jokes.text: George Michael starts to say 'Mr. Manager' again and Michael cuts him off: 'Manager. We just say...' / 'I know, but you just...' / 'It doesn't matter who.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just corrected George Michael once already about 'Mr. Manager'
jokes.punchline: George Michael immediately repeats it again; Michael's pre-emptive correction and 'It doesn't matter who' dismissal
jokes.explanation: The immediate escalation of the Mr. Manager gag works because Michael's frustration has already grown and his 'It doesn't matter who' is a perfect dismissive button. The rapid repeat of the gag before the scene changes is good sitcom economy.

jokes.text: George Michael says 'I'm Mr. Manager' again; Michael starts to correct him; George Michael cuts him off: 'I know, but you just...' Michael: 'It doesn't matter who.'
jokes.setup: Michael has already corrected George Michael about 'Mr. Manager' twice
jokes.punchline: George Michael preemptively acknowledges the correction but still can't stop himself; Michael gives up entirely with 'It doesn't matter who' — a perfect surrender
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the Mr. Manager gag to its natural conclusion — total parental exhaustion. 'It doesn't matter who' is a perfectly defeated non-answer that shows Michael has already stopped caring. Good structural payoff to the earlier setup.

jokes.text: Michael arrives home; Lindsay and Tobias are in the exact same positions as when he left that morning. 'Is nobody gonna even try to get a job?'
jokes.setup: The narrator sets up that Michael left them that morning; he returns to find nothing has changed
jokes.punchline: The visual of them in literally identical positions — the joke is the complete absence of any effort or movement from either of them all day
jokes.explanation: A strong visual-structural gag. The frozen tableau of Lindsay and Tobias is a perfect visual metaphor for the Bluth family's paralysis. Michael's exasperated question lands well because the visual says everything first. High character integration — Lindsay and Tobias's spectacular laziness is a core character trait.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I have a job, Michael. It's called supporting my husband.' Michael: 'You haven't been shopping.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay claims she has a job — 'supporting my husband'
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately deflates this by noting she hasn't even done the one thing that would constitute 'support' — shopping — undercutting her self-justification
jokes.explanation: Clean two-liner. Lindsay's grandiose self-justification ('it's called supporting my husband') followed by Michael's one-sentence demolition of it is efficient and character-true. The joke works because Lindsay sincerely believes her framing and Michael cuts right through it.

jokes.text: The dead dove in the freezer — Lucille trying to return it to a pet store
jokes.setup: Michael mentions he found a dead dove in a bag in the freezer
jokes.punchline: Lucille panics that he might have eaten it because she 'only has a couple days left to return it' — she is attempting to return a dead animal to a pet store for a refund
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully dark and absurdist. The idea of a dead dove preserved for return to a pet store is macabre, but Lucille's matter-of-fact delivery makes it the logical thing to do. 'Died right in the middle of a show' adds a layer (it's a magician's dove), and the payoff in the tag with Gob's frozen dove deepens the gag further. High character integration — Lucille's imperious refusal to absorb losses, even from a dead bird, is perfectly her. The 'Do you want a cage for that? / No. I'm a magician.' exchange is a tight button.

jokes.text: Lucille asking about the return policy for the dead dove
jokes.setup: Lucille is at a pet store trying to return a dove that died in a magic act
jokes.punchline: 'What is your return policy, by the way?' — asked with complete sincerity about a dead bird, as if this is a normal customer service inquiry
jokes.explanation: A good button on the dead dove joke. Lucille's casual 'by the way' frames her question as an afterthought, which is funnier than if she led with it. The joke is carried entirely by character — Lucille treating this as routine consumer behavior.

jokes.text: Lindsay spent $68 on hair conditioner; 'A small price to pay for self-esteem, Michael. Or are you still jealous that you lost Best Hair to me in high school and got Dorkiest?'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Lindsay about spending $68 on hair conditioner instead of food
jokes.punchline: Lindsay escalates her self-justification to 'self-esteem,' then weaponizes Michael's high school yearbook superlative ('Dorkiest') to deflect
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed two-step joke. The $68 conditioner is absurd enough, but Lindsay's pivot to 'self-esteem' and then the surprise attack with the 'Dorkiest' superlative is a sharp escalation. It also plants a seed the narrator immediately waters with the next beat. High character integration — Lindsay's weaponized vanity and casual cruelty to Michael are signature traits.

jokes.text: Narrator confirms: 'Lindsay's achievements in high school had always overshadowed Michael's'
jokes.setup: Lindsay just claimed her 'Best Hair' win over Michael's 'Dorkiest'
jokes.punchline: The narrator validates this absurd superlative rivalry as if it were a serious academic achievement gap — deadpan affirmation of the ridiculous
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan confirmation is a great example of the show's narrative voice as a comedy tool. By treating 'Best Hair' as a legitimate academic achievement, the narration amplifies the absurdity without editorializing. Functional and well-placed, but not exceptional on its own.

jokes.text: Tobias delivers a speech about rejection in the 'business of show'... but has never actually had an audition
jokes.setup: Tobias agrees with Michael about not tying self-esteem to looks, and launches into a passionate speech about rejection in acting
jokes.punchline: Michael quietly observes: 'But you've never actually had an audition.' — the grand speech about enduring rejection is revealed to be entirely theoretical
jokes.explanation: Perfectly structured Tobias joke. The buildup — 'the heart of an angel and the hide of an elephant' — is florid enough to be funny on its own, but the punchline ('you've never actually had an audition') retroactively makes the whole speech pathetic and hilarious. This is quintessential Tobias: maximum delusion, zero self-awareness. Strong character integration — this joke only works because of Tobias's specific brand of pompous incompetence.

jokes.text: Tobias's indignant 'Well... excuse me!' after being told he's never had an audition, followed by another 'Excuse me.' as he leaves
jokes.setup: Michael has just deflated Tobias's grand speech by pointing out he's never had an audition
jokes.punchline: Tobias's response is an over-dramatic exit punctuated by two separate 'Excuse me's — the second being funnier because it continues the bit unnecessarily
jokes.explanation: A good button on the Tobias joke. The double 'excuse me' works because the second one — separated by a beat — extends the wounded dignity past the point of normal social interaction. The physical comedy of his exit adds to it. Functional character work.

jokes.text: Michael lectures Lindsay about setting an example for Maeby; Lindsay replies 'Yeah. Shoot me when that happens.' / Michael: 'Is there a carbon monoxide leak in this house?'
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Lindsay might want to set a better example so Maeby doesn't 'end up just like you'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's 'Shoot me when that happens' accepts her daughter becoming her as a fate worse than death; Michael's 'carbon monoxide' line suggests the family's collective dysfunction is a toxin, not a character flaw
jokes.explanation: Two distinct jokes in quick succession. Lindsay's casual self-deprecation ('Shoot me') is dark but character-appropriate — she's the only one with any ironic self-awareness about the family. Michael's carbon monoxide line is a nice absurdist pivot that reframes the scene as environmentally impossible rather than merely dysfunctional.

jokes.text: Michael worries about Maeby working at the banana stand: 'I don't know about this. I mean, it can get pretty hairy in there.'
jokes.setup: Michael is concerned about Maeby working at the banana stand
jokes.punchline: 'Pretty hairy' — standard idiom for 'difficult/risky,' but also literally refers to the hair conditioner subplot and Lindsay's 'Best Hair' win; works as an inadvertent pun
jokes.explanation: A mild pun that functions as a throwaway line. The 'hairy' wordplay is there but not strongly enough constructed to be a standout joke. It gets the scene from A to B with a mild smile.

jokes.text: George Sr.'s advice to Michael about Maeby: 'You stay on top of her, buddy. Do not be afraid to ride her. Hard.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is advising Michael to be strict with Maeby as her boss at the banana stand
jokes.punchline: The workplace-management advice ('ride her hard') is phrased in a way that sounds completely inappropriate, delivered with total obliviousness by George Sr.
jokes.explanation: A classic double-entendre gag. 'Ride her hard' as management advice about a teenage niece is intentionally uncomfortable. The escalation through three separate phrases ('stay on top of her,' 'ride her,' 'hard') is well-structured, each phrase worse than the last. Character-appropriate for George Sr.'s oblivious bluntness.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George Michael's attempt to distance himself from his cousin proved unsuccessful.' — George Michael and Maeby are literally at the banana stand together
jokes.setup: George Michael took the banana stand job specifically to get away from Maeby
jokes.punchline: Michael assigned Maeby as his employee, so his escape is now his entrapment — the narrator confirms the irony with perfect economy
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan efficiency is perfect here. The joke is entirely structural — the irony of George Michael's plan backfiring is set up by his earlier motivation (escaping cramped van with Maeby) and paid off visually. The narration adds a wry button without over-explaining.

jokes.text: Maeby's scheme to drain the cash register by giving away bananas for free: 'Banana. Buck. Banana. Take a buck.'
jokes.setup: George Michael explains that bananas sold must match money in the drawer
jokes.punchline: Maeby's immediate solution is to take a banana and take a dollar simultaneously — she has understood the system perfectly in order to abuse it perfectly
jokes.explanation: A beautifully economical joke. Maeby's instant comprehension and immediate exploitation of the loophole is perfectly character-specific — she's the show's most naturally devious character. 'Banana. Buck.' as her casual theft rhythm is quotable and funny. The beat where George Michael has to acknowledge 'It's fine' shows his complicity. High character integration.

jokes.text: Gob calling from the ocean, apparently calling Michael while he's clearly dumping the letter: 'Then why don't you marry an ice cream sandwich?'
jokes.setup: Michael is on the phone; Gob's side of a separate conversation is heard in passing
jokes.punchline: 'Then why don't you marry an ice cream sandwich?' — Gob's non-sequitur response to whoever he's talking to is completely context-free and childishly absurd
jokes.explanation: A quick absurdist joke that works on multiple levels. 'Why don't you marry it?' is a childish schoolyard retort; applied to an ice cream sandwich, it's wonderfully specific and stupid. The callback to George Sr.'s ice cream sandwich love affair rewards attentive viewers. Gob's complete obliviousness about who he's insulting adds to the joke.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I don't know where they are.' (before Michael has finished his sentence) — Michael: 'You know, it's really more believable if you let me finish.'
jokes.setup: Michael visits his mother to ask about the flight records
jokes.punchline: Lucille denies knowing something before Michael even says what it is, revealing she already knows exactly what he's asking and is lying — Michael's dry correction is the punchline
jokes.explanation: Exceptionally efficient comedy. Lucille pre-emptively denies knowledge of something she hasn't been asked about — a perfect distillation of her character: guilty, controlling, and unable to even perform innocence convincingly. Michael's 'more believable if you let me finish' is a masterful straight-man line that's funnier for its reasonableness. This is close to a benchmark joke — high originality in execution, perfect economy, and could only be Lucille Bluth.

jokes.text: Lucille's increasingly vague description of the storage unit location: 'Something with Dale... Brookfeather... Raintree... It's hot. It's very hot there. I've never been. Get a warrant!'
jokes.setup: Michael asks where the storage unit is; Lucille claims not to know
jokes.punchline: Lucille's escalating specificity ('Raintree,' 'It's hot') betrays her knowledge, culminating in the absurd legal assertion 'Get a warrant!' — she's describing a place she claims never to have visited in detail
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best comedic sequences. The joke escalates through four beats: vague name guesses → increasingly specific guesses → sensory detail ('It's hot') → 'I've never been' (which contradicts all the detail) → 'Get a warrant!' (a legal defense for a conversation). Lucille's guilty self-incrimination through over-description is brilliant, and 'Get a warrant!' is a perfect absurdist button that has become a fan-favorite quote. High character integration and originality.

jokes.text: 'It's hot. It's very hot there. I've never been.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is supposedly trying to remember the name of a storage unit she claims never to have visited
jokes.punchline: Lucille volunteers that the storage unit 'is hot' and 'very hot' — specific sensory detail that immediately undermines her claim to have 'never been.' She's caught herself in real time.
jokes.explanation: Excellent construction — the 'It's hot. It's very hot there' is played as genuine recollection, and then Lucille's instinct to cover it immediately with 'I've never been' creates a perfect self-incriminating beat. The rapid correction is the payoff. Structure is tight and character integration is total.

jokes.text: Michael: 'But he's not special, Mother.' — a sincere assessment of Gob delivered with complete seriousness
jokes.setup: Lucille pleads for Michael to give Gob a role because he's special
jokes.punchline: Michael's blunt 'He's not special, Mother' is said with complete conviction — no cruelty intended, just honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfectly delivered understatement. Michael's deadpan 'He's not special, Mother' is funny because it's the most direct possible response to maternal pleading, delivered without apparent awareness of how harsh it sounds. The joke is entirely in the economy and tone — four words demolish Lucille's request.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Where's the storage unit?' / Lucille: 'It's with your warrant.'
jokes.setup: Michael again asks where the storage unit is after a brief emotional interlude
jokes.punchline: 'It's with your warrant' — a callback to her earlier 'Get a warrant!' that turns her legal deflection into a geography joke
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback structure. 'It's with your warrant' takes the earlier absurdist legal non-sequitur and sharpens it into a perfect put-down. Lucille refuses to be worn down — she converts her earlier defensive exclamation into a composed witticism. High economy (four words, complete joke), and perfectly in character. Strong case for top-tier joke in the episode.

jokes.text: Lucille to her housekeeper Luz: 'Luz, that coat cost more than your house!' / 'Oh, that's how we joke. She doesn't even have a house.'
jokes.setup: Lucille casually says something cruel to her housekeeper
jokes.punchline: Her aside to Michael — 'that's how we joke, she doesn't even have a house' — reveals the 'joke' is actually a sincere statement of class contempt, and she's proud of this
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most revealing and darkly funny moments. The structure is perfect: Lucille makes a cruel joke, then immediately explains to Michael that it's not actually a joke — which makes it MORE horrifying AND funnier simultaneously. 'She doesn't even have a house' delivered as a fond clarification is peak Lucille Bluth. High character integration: this joke could only be Lucille, and it tells you everything about her in two sentences. 'That's how we joke' is already an excellent Arrested Development-style line.

jokes.text: Tobias stares longingly at an 'Actor Pull' classified ad — the visual of this sad moment
jokes.setup: Narrator says Tobias 'looked for work'
jokes.punchline: He is seen staring at a single classified listing called 'Actor Pull' — the implication being that he found one listing and was overwhelmed, and/or that an 'actor pull' is not even a real acting job
jokes.explanation: A visual gag that works mostly through implication and Tobias's earnest enthusiasm for minimal opportunity. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing Tobias's full arc — this is early evidence of his delusion being matched only by his incompetence. Moderate scoring; this is more setup than punchline.

jokes.text: 'I was looking for work, and I blew it, Michael.' / 'I guess you've already seen that, huh?' / 'Yes, I have. Oh, right. This one.'
jokes.setup: Tobias tells Michael he 'blew it' looking for work
jokes.punchline: The exchange about whether Michael has 'already seen' it implies Tobias's failure to find work was somehow visible/documented — the matter-of-fact 'Yes, I have. Oh, right. This one.' from Michael suggests Tobias's failures are so regular they're a known quantity
jokes.explanation: Mildly funny — Michael's 'Oh, right. This one.' implies a catalogue of Tobias failures he's learned to recognize by type. Underplayed but effective.

jokes.text: Michael tricks Lucille by calling and mentioning the IRS wants to check for 'furs or...' — then following her housekeeper Luz to the storage unit
jokes.setup: Michael can't get his mother to reveal where the storage unit is
jokes.punchline: He calls with a fake IRS warning about 'furs' — knowing this will panic Lucille into sending Luz to hide evidence — revealing that his mother is so predictably corrupt that he can manipulate her by mentioning luxury goods
jokes.explanation: A smart structural joke that works through character logic. Michael has figured out his mother so completely that he can predict her illegal behavior and exploit it. The comedy is in how completely unsurprised we are that this works — and the narrator's 'Oh, that poor woman' (referring to Luz) adds an ironic button. Good craft, slightly diluted by the scene spanning multiple beats.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Oh, that poor woman.' — referring to Luz being sent on an errand, not to Lucille
jokes.setup: Michael just tricked Lucille; Lucille is presumably outraged
jokes.punchline: The narrator's sympathy is for Luz the housekeeper, not Lucille — the person being 'wronged' by the trick — because Lucille is so unsympathetic that even the narrator won't side with her
jokes.explanation: A perfect five-word joke from the narrator. The misdirect is elegant — we expect sympathy for Lucille being tricked, but the narrator redirects it to Luz, who has to actually do the running around. It's also a commentary on Lucille's character: she's so awful that the omniscient narrator takes sides against her. Economy score is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Tobias at the commercial audition: 'Not if I do my job right' when told the audition is just one line
jokes.setup: Lindsay says the audition is just one line; Tobias replies confidently
jokes.punchline: 'Not if I do my job right' — Tobias believes his acting so transforms material that one line becomes many; this is delusional grandiosity delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: A compact, perfectly structured Tobias joke. The setup is neutral (one line), and Tobias's response reframes it as an artistic challenge — his confidence is completely unjustified and reveals everything about his delusion. 'Not if I do my job right' could be said by a great actor; from Tobias, it's devastatingly funny because we know what's coming. High economy, very high character integration. One of the better jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Tobias asks whether he should be 'panicked about the fire' or 'being brave for everyone else' — it's an ad for a fire sale
jokes.setup: Tobias questions the emotional motivation for the commercial
jokes.punchline: The director clarifies it's a fire sale — a retail event — and Tobias has completely misread the script as a disaster drama
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed misdirection built on a pun. 'Fire sale' has two meanings; Tobias has chosen the dramatic one. His question about whether he's 'being brave for everyone else' during what he thinks is a disaster scenario — while auditioning for a furniture/clothing ad — is perfectly absurd. The joke escalates into his actual performance, making this a strong setup for what follows.

jokes.text: Tobias performs the fire sale commercial: 'Oh, my God! We're having a fire... sale.' / 'Oh, the burning! It burns me!' / 'Evacuate all the schoolchildren!' / 'This isn't a fever!'
jokes.setup: Tobias is doing a commercial audition for a fire sale at South Coast Boutique
jokes.punchline: He performs the one-line script as a full disaster drama — screaming about burning, evacuating schoolchildren, confusing it with a fever — an absolutely unhinged escalating performance
jokes.explanation: The episode's most purely funny sequence. Tobias's audition is a masterpiece of escalating absurdity — each line worse than the last, building from overwrought ('Oh, the burning!') to completely unhinged ('Evacuate all the schoolchildren!') to medically confused ('This isn't a fever!'). The perfect structure: setup the pun misunderstanding, then deliver the consequences in full. David Cross's physical performance is implied to be spectacular. 'Evacuate all the schoolchildren' is a line from another scene entirely — Tobias has written his own disaster movie from a two-word brief. Near the top of the episode's jokes. The 'And scene' from the director landing after all this is a perfect button.

jokes.text: 'I can't even see where the knob is!' — Tobias, during the fire sale audition
jokes.setup: Tobias is in the middle of his unhinged fire sale performance
jokes.punchline: He's apparently searching for a door or stove knob in his imagined fire scenario — the specificity of 'where the knob is' makes the absurdism more vivid
jokes.explanation: A good escalation beat within the audition sequence. The specific detail of 'the knob' grounds the absurdity and extends the joke without repeating it. Tobias has clearly invented an entire burning-building scenario that includes searching for heat controls. Scored separately from the main audition sequence as a distinct escalation beat.

jokes.text: Director after Tobias's performance: 'Would you like to try that a little simpler maybe?' / Tobias: 'No.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just delivered an operatically unhinged one-line audition
jokes.punchline: The director's devastated understatement ('a little simpler') is funny on its own; Tobias's one-word refusal is a perfect punchline button
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect exchange. The director's 'a little simpler maybe?' is already funny for its desperate understatement after witnessing a disaster. Tobias's flat 'No.' — a complete refusal to course-correct, delivered with the confidence of someone who knows they've given a great performance — is exceptional economy. Ten words total (including the setup), and the joke lands fully. 'No.' as a standalone punchline to that setup is as economical as comedy gets.

jokes.text: After Tobias's audition implodes, the director immediately calls for 'Lindsay Bluth' — her ex-boyfriend from high school who has moved on to casting commercials
jokes.setup: Tobias has just destroyed his audition; the director needs someone else
jokes.punchline: He calls for Lindsay, who turns out to be an old flame — the irony that Tobias just auditioned for and lost a job to his own wife's ex-boyfriend is the structural joke
jokes.explanation: A nicely layered ironic setup. The Tobias/Lindsay/Roger Danish triangle pays off the 'Best Hair' subplot and adds humiliation to Tobias's already catastrophic audition. It also establishes a new subplot (Lindsay getting the job). The Roger Danish name is funny on its own — 'Roger Danish' sounds aggressively forgettable.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Roger reminisce about their 'crazy hair' in high school while Tobias is in the background
jokes.setup: Roger and Lindsay reconnect as old high school friends
jokes.punchline: They immediately bond over hair — directly calling back to the 'Best Hair' superlative — while Tobias presumably watches his wife flirt with the man who just rejected him
jokes.explanation: A functional callback that deepens the joke economy. The 'hair' reunion conversation directly references the earlier subplot, and the situational irony (Tobias's humiliation compounded by his wife hitting it off with the casting director) is well-constructed. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: The storage unit is on fire when Michael arrives — after following Luz all the way there
jokes.setup: Michael has elaborately tricked Lucille and followed Luz to finally find the storage unit
jokes.punchline: It's already on fire — his entire scheme yields nothing because someone got there first
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed structural joke. Michael's elaborate plan — fake IRS call, following the housekeeper across town — pays off with complete futility. The Arrested Development formula of 'elaborate effort → immediate reversal' is perfectly executed. Narrator's 'unfortunately, it was on fire' is delivered with characteristic understatement. Rewatch bonus because the whole episode's arson plot clicks into place here.

jokes.text: Fire investigator: 'Oh, definitely the work of a flamer.' — about the arson
jokes.setup: Michael is looking at the burned storage unit and asks if it's arson
jokes.punchline: 'Definitely the work of a flamer' — which now means both the slang term AND 'an arsonist,' paying off the T-Bone joke from earlier
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's best long-form joke. 'Definitely the work of a flamer' lands on three levels: (1) the fire investigator uses professional jargon for an arsonist, (2) the slang meaning is still present, (3) it confirms T-Bone as the arsonist and retroactively validates both usages of 'flamer' from earlier. This is textbook Arrested Development — a joke that works on first watch and becomes richer every time. Near the episode's peak for craft.

jokes.text: Tobias explains he 'made the fire too real' and 'probably failed to highlight the sale'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has gotten the commercial job that Tobias auditioned for
jokes.punchline: Tobias's self-assessment — that he 'made the fire too real' and didn't emphasize the sale — is simultaneously the most accurate and most deluded analysis of his performance possible
jokes.explanation: A perfect example of Tobias's specific flavor of delusion. His critique — 'made the fire too real' — accepts his interpretation of the script as correct and only faults his emphasis. He doesn't consider that evacuating schoolchildren is wrong; he considers it slightly unbalanced. 'Probably failed to highlight the sale' is magnificently understated.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, isn't that ironic? I'm making a fortune at my new job, and you don't even have the job you thought you did.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob the flight records burned; Gob gloats
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately deflates Gob: 'But you don't have a job. You've got a job offer. Anybody can get a job offer.' — Gob's 'fortune' is equally fictional
jokes.explanation: A nicely symmetrical joke structure. Gob gloats about Michael's failed position — then Michael reveals Gob's 'fortune' is just an offer, not a job, making them equally unemployed. 'Anybody can get a job offer' is a sharp, deflating line. High character integration — Michael's exacting precision about what constitutes 'having a job' is distinctly his.

jokes.text: Gob, instead of helping Michael, asks him to mail a letter — 'You can't just give that to a mailman? / I can't trust a mailman with this. This is important.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Gob for help; Gob asks Michael to mail a letter instead
jokes.punchline: Gob's counter-request is to mail a letter — the most basic possible errand — and he doesn't trust the postal service with it, implying it's sensitive, while he's asking Michael to do it on a street corner
jokes.explanation: Good Gob character work. His inability to do the simplest task (mailing a letter) and his paranoia about the postal service delivering what he considers sensitive material is perfectly absurd. Rewatch bonus because the letter's fate — thrown dramatically into the sea — pays this off beautifully.

jokes.text: Narrator: Gob 'couldn't return a completely frozen dove to a pet store and get the full refund he felt he was entitled to'
jokes.setup: Gob agrees to mail the letter; the narrator explains why he's really doing it
jokes.punchline: Gob's real motivation is avoiding the awkward conversation at the pet store about returning a frozen dead dove — he can't even accomplish this simple return, so mailing the letter is his way of delaying
jokes.explanation: The narrator illuminates Gob's hidden motivation with perfect deadpan. 'The full refund he felt he was entitled to' for a frozen dead dove is a beautiful encapsulation of Gob's delusional entitlement. It also deepens the dead dove running gag. Rewatch bonus because the 'frozen dove' callback connects to Lucille's dead dove from the same source.

jokes.text: George Sr., hearing that George Michael is running the banana stand: 'George Michael is running the frozen banana stand now?' / 'That's right. Yeah. You sit with that. You think about that for a while.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Sr. he put George Michael in charge of the banana stand as a power move
jokes.punchline: 'You sit with that. You think about that for a while.' — Michael's triumphant takedown is immediately undercut by George Sr. silently realizing this is a problem (George Sr. knows the $250k is in the walls)
jokes.explanation: A structural comedy masterpiece on rewatch. Michael's triumphant 'you sit with that' is meant to be his power move — but George Sr.'s reaction (silently freaking out because the kid with the matches is now running the building full of $250k) gives the scene a completely different meaning on second viewing. 'George Sr. did think about that' from the narrator, followed by his visible alarm, is the real punchline. The joke deepens enormously with the episode's revelation.

jokes.text: George Sr. visibly panics after Michael leaves — 'George Sr. did think about that'
jokes.setup: Michael has smugly told George Sr. that his teenage son is running the banana stand — which has $250k in the walls
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s visible alarm is the punchline — Michael thinks he's won, but George Sr. is panicking because the banana stand has just become a liability
jokes.explanation: A near-silent visual punchline of exceptional quality. The narrator's flat 'George Sr. did think about that' is a brilliantly understated button on what we're seeing: a man realizing his own son has unknowingly endangered $250,000 in cash. On first watch, it's a mildly funny reaction shot. On rewatch, it's devastating dramatic irony. The rewatch bonus is enormous.

jokes.text: George Michael's employee math: 'We throw away a banana for every buck we take so no one finds out.' / 'Wait, I think you should do that math again.' / 'Is it wrong?' / 'It's fine.'
jokes.setup: George Michael explains their coverup scheme to Maeby
jokes.punchline: Maeby clearly realizes the scheme makes no sense (throwing away a banana AND taking a dollar means they're losing twice), but George Michael thinks it's fine — the joke being he's running a business he doesn't fully understand
jokes.explanation: A quietly funny math joke. George Michael's scheme has a fundamental flaw he doesn't see; Maeby asks him to check the math; he confirms he doesn't understand that it's wrong. The 'It's fine' from Maeby as she sees an opportunity is also a character-true beat. Good but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'He's an arsonist, not an embezzler.' — about T-Bone working the cash register
jokes.setup: T-Bone is working the cash register at the banana stand
jokes.punchline: The narrator reassures us that T-Bone won't steal the money — because his crime specialty is fire, not theft — as if this is a reasonable distinction and reassurance
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest narrator jokes. 'He's an arsonist, not an embezzler' is delivered as genuine reassurance — as if the relevant concern about having a criminal run your cash register is merely misclassifying his specialty. The economy is exceptional: seven words, complete joke, big laugh. It also works as a callback to the entire T-Bone/flamer arc. Near the top of the episode.

jokes.text: Gob 'dramatically hurled the letter into the sea' — which proved 'a more difficult dramatic gesture than he'd anticipated'
jokes.setup: Gob, in an act of defiance, decides to throw the letter into the ocean instead of mailing it
jokes.punchline: The dramatic gesture fails — the letter doesn't go far, or Gob struggles with it — 'this proved a more difficult dramatic gesture than he'd anticipated'
jokes.explanation: A beautiful Gob character comedy moment. The joke has three layers: (1) the absurdity of Gob dramatically hurling mail into the sea as rebellion, (2) his failure to even do that properly, and (3) the later revelation that the letter was actually the insurance check — the plot consequence of his petty dramatic gesture becomes the episode's darkest punchline. Narrator's understatement ('a more difficult dramatic gesture than he'd anticipated') is perfect. Rewatch bonus is enormous because the insurance check revelation recontextualizes everything about this scene.

jokes.text: Lucille's response to Michael's complaint about Gob: 'He's my son. I want you to make him stop calling me.'
jokes.setup: Lucille calls Michael concerned about Gob; Michael asks what she wants him to do
jokes.punchline: Lucille's maternal concern is entirely about getting Gob to stop calling her — she doesn't want to help him, she wants silence
jokes.explanation: A compact Lucille joke that perfectly captures her brand of maternal dysfunction. She introduces the call as concern for her son, then reveals her concern is entirely self-serving — she wants the calling to stop. 'He's my son' followed immediately by wanting him to stop contacting her is a sharp reversal. High character integration.

jokes.text: Tobias declares: 'Who would have imagined — two actors in one family? We're like the Lunts!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just gotten the commercial job that Tobias failed to get
jokes.punchline: Tobias celebrates them as 'the Lunts' — a legendary acting couple — while he has never successfully completed an audition and she just got a job for reasons entirely unrelated to acting ability
jokes.explanation: A Tobias joke that rewards viewers who catch the reference. The Lunts (Alfred Lunt and Lynn Fontanne) were a legendary Broadway couple. Tobias's comparison of himself and Lindsay to theatrical royalty — while he just bombed a fire sale audition — is perfectly deluded. Character-specific and has a layer for informed viewers.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Maeby both celebrate jobs they didn't actually do, at the same restaurant, with unearned money
jokes.setup: Lindsay got the commercial; Maeby 'worked' at the banana stand
jokes.punchline: Narrator: 'Lindsay and Maeby separately went to the same restaurant to celebrate the jobs they hadn't actually performed, with money they hadn't actually earned'
jokes.explanation: The narrator's comprehensive summary is a good structural joke — 'jobs they hadn't performed with money they hadn't earned' is efficient and perfectly describes the Bluth family pathology. The fact that mother and daughter independently made the same terrible choice and ended up in the same place is a satisfying parallel structure.

jokes.text: Lucille to Lindsay at the restaurant: 'Not as much as you enjoyed yours. You want your belt to buckle, not your chair.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay makes a passive-aggressive remark about Lucille drinking fast; Lucille responds
jokes.punchline: 'You want your belt to buckle, not your chair' — a devastating fat joke from Lucille delivered with casual cruelty
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly crafted Lucille insult. The joke structure — 'you want your belt to buckle, not your chair' — is a genuine piece of comic writing: 'buckle' as a verb works for both belt and chair, and the parallel construction is elegant. It's also perfectly mean and perfectly Lucille. High economy, high character integration. One of the better lines in the episode.

jokes.text: George Michael's math realization: 'For every dollar you take, you're actually taking two dollars because we paid for the bananas.'
jokes.setup: George Michael reconsiders their banana-and-money scheme
jokes.punchline: He realizes they've been doubling their losses — and then immediately runs to stop it, with Maeby validating his panic
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the banana math subplot. George Michael's sudden comprehension ('Oh, God, I've screwed this up!') is a good character moment — he's been complicit in something he didn't fully understand. The joke is more plot-functional than comedic, but the buildup to it is well-structured.

jokes.text: George Michael and Maeby panic at seeing their mothers at the same restaurant — 'Oh, my God! It's your mom and Gangy.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby are caught in the restaurant celebrating stolen banana stand money
jokes.punchline: The very people they're trying to avoid (who have also spent unearned money) are at the same restaurant doing the same thing — but the kids are scared of being caught while the mothers are oblivious
jokes.explanation: A functional farce setup — two groups at the same restaurant, one trying to hide. The irony that the mothers are equally guilty adds a layer, but the scene is more plot mechanics than comedy. 'Gangy' as a nickname for Lucille is a nice character detail.

jokes.text: 'Pop-pop, he's a businessman. What would he do?' / 'Bananas Foster.'
jokes.setup: George Michael wonders what their corrupt grandfather would do to cover up financial irregularities
jokes.punchline: 'Bananas Foster' — a dessert made with flambéed bananas, which they use to burn the evidence (via a flaming dessert), but also a pun on 'Bananas Foster' as a legal/financial term (like 'foster care' or 'fostering growth')
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. 'Bananas Foster' works on multiple levels: it's a literal dessert they can order to create fire/distraction, it's a pun on the banana business, and it calls back to the show's central metaphor of bananas as the family's business. The economy is exceptional — two words, and everyone understands the plan. 'What would he do?' → 'Bananas Foster' is one of the best setups/punchlines in the episode. The joke rewards multiple viewings as you appreciate more fully that this is both a plan AND an homage to Pop-pop's criminal methods.

jokes.text: Lucille to Maeby about the flaming Bananas Foster: 'You might wanna let that fire go out before you stick your face in it.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has ordered Bananas Foster (a flaming dessert) as a distraction/cover scheme
jokes.punchline: Lucille's casual warning is simultaneously grandmotherly advice and a callback to her earlier physical-appearance humor
jokes.explanation: A decent Lucille line that bridges the Bananas Foster joke to the next beat. The advice is practical but delivered with Lucille's characteristic blend of concern-and-condescension. Sets up the better joke that follows.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'That's funny, 'cause I was gonna say, You might wanna lean away from that fire since you're soaked in alcohol.' / Lucille: 'Mine was better.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has just given fire safety advice to Maeby; Maeby counters with a burn about Lucille's drinking
jokes.punchline: Lucille's response — 'Mine was better' — is both an admission of the drinking AND a refusal to be embarrassed, delivered with complete composure
jokes.explanation: One of the best exchanges in the episode. Maeby's counter-burn about Lucille being 'soaked in alcohol' is good on its own, but Lucille's response is the real joke: 'Mine was better' is simultaneously an acknowledgment that she is indeed soaked in alcohol AND a refusal to cede the point. It's an impeccably timed non-denial denial delivered with supreme confidence. Lucille's character is perfectly distilled in two words. 'Mine was better' is instantly quotable and captures Lucille's essence as well as anything in the episode.

jokes.text: George Michael's banana stand pitch to a customer: 'Welcome to Bluth Bananas, where bananas are our business. May I interest you in a banana this day?'
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to sell bananas at the stand
jokes.punchline: 'May I interest you in a banana this day?' — absurdly formal for a beach snack stand; 'this day' instead of 'today' is an archaic phrasing that suggests George Michael has over-prepared
jokes.explanation: A good George Michael character moment. His overly formal pitch ('this day,' 'bananas are our business') shows his desperate earnestness to do the job right. Character-specific and charming.

jokes.text: T-Bone appears at the banana stand: 'Your dad gave me this job.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the banana stand and finds T-Bone working there
jokes.punchline: 'Your dad gave me this job' — George Sr. has already found a way to place his arsonist prison roommate in Michael's business, proving he's still running things from prison
jokes.explanation: A satisfying payoff of multiple threads. George Sr. gets T-Bone a job despite Michael's refusal — proving George Sr.'s ability to control events from prison — and it's specifically at the banana stand, the place most at risk from an arsonist. The structural irony (an arsonist working at the banana stand full of cash, which then burns) is layered. 'Your dad gave me this job' is a great deadpan line.

jokes.text: Michael asks T-Bone 'You burn down the storage unit?' T-Bone: 'Oh, most definitely.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts T-Bone about the arson
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, most definitely.' — T-Bone confirms he burned down the storage unit with complete casual cheerfulness, as if Michael had asked about his weekend plans
jokes.explanation: The perfect deadpan confession. 'Oh, most definitely' is the ideal response — enthusiastic, unhesitant, and completely unbothered by the criminal implications. The economy is superb. The gap between the gravity of the crime and T-Bone's cheerful confirmation is the joke.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Michael, having a nice day at the beach while the rest of us are busting our asses to deliver your mail?'
jokes.setup: Gob is supposed to be mailing a letter; he finds Michael at the beach
jokes.punchline: Gob has turned mailing one letter into 'busting our asses' — the grandiosity of his complaint about the easiest possible errand
jokes.explanation: Gob's victim mentality is perfectly captured here. 'Busting our asses to deliver your mail' about a single letter he hasn't actually mailed is a great Gob line. The 'our asses' (as if he has a team) also implies he's been complaining to others about this. Rewatch bonus because we know he threw it in the sea.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I should be in charge. I'm the older brother.' / 'Please, do you even wanna be in charge?' / 'No! But I'd like to be asked.'
jokes.setup: Gob argues he should run the company because he's older
jokes.punchline: When Michael cuts to the real question — do you even want it? — Gob immediately admits he doesn't, but then pivots to his real grievance: not being asked
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a defining Gob moment for the entire series. The structure is perfect: thesis (I should be in charge) → challenge (do you even want it?) → surprising admission (No!) → real complaint (but I'd like to be asked). The joke reveals Gob's entire character — he doesn't want responsibility, he wants the appearance of being considered. 'No! But I'd like to be asked' is a line that could only come from Gob Bluth and perfectly encapsulates a certain type of fragile ego. Exceptionally high character integration, structure, and quotability. Comparable to the reference anchors provided.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Better than being treated like the goofball, the joker, the magician.' — then attempts a fireball that doesn't go off
jokes.setup: Gob is complaining about being underestimated; he's positioning himself as more than a performer
jokes.punchline: In the middle of his complaint about being dismissed as 'the magician,' Gob attempts a fireball magic trick for emphasis — and it fails — Michael: 'I thought you were gonna do, like, a trick there.' Gob: 'I was. It didn't go off.'
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. Gob is in the middle of a sincere, emotionally resonant complaint about being reduced to 'the magician' — and then immediately, involuntarily demonstrates exactly why he's treated that way by attempting a fireball trick. The timing is perfect: 'the joker, the magician' [attempts trick] [fails]. Michael's underplayed 'I thought you were gonna do, like, a trick there' and Gob's devastated 'I was. It didn't go off. These things never go off when you want them to. My least consistent trick.' are all gold. This joke is structurally perfect: argument → physical evidence of the argument → dignified acceptance. Pure character comedy at its highest level. Character integration is a 10 — this is Gob Bluth in one scene.

jokes.text: 'These things never go off when you want them to. My least consistent trick.'
jokes.setup: Gob's fireball trick has just failed in the middle of his speech about being a real magician
jokes.punchline: 'My least consistent trick' — Gob's professional assessment of his own unreliable fireball is delivered with the calm authority of an expert discussing a technical limitation, when the limitation is 'it usually doesn't work'
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan understatement. 'My least consistent trick' frames 'it often just doesn't work' as a professional performance metric. The fact that Gob is actively trying to prove he's a real magician while describing a trick that rarely functions is the peak of the joke.

jokes.text: Maeby calls Michael to rat out George Michael: 'He's about to do something really irresponsible.' / Michael: 'You think it's irresponsible? I'll be right there.'
jokes.setup: Maeby warns Michael that George Michael is about to burn down the banana stand
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate 'I'll be right there' is presented as parental concern but is also the fastest he's moved all episode — he's more responsive to 'irresponsible' than to any crisis
jokes.explanation: A functional transitional joke. Maeby's calculated betrayal of George Michael (to avoid blame) is character-true, and Michael's immediate response contrasts his general paralysis throughout the episode. Not a standout moment but necessary plot and character work.

jokes.text: George Michael, caught burning the banana stand: 'I was just, uh... I was burning down the banana stand.'
jokes.setup: Michael catches George Michael at the burning banana stand and asks what he's doing
jokes.punchline: 'I was just, uh... I was burning down the banana stand' — the 'uh' suggests he was going to cover but can't find any explanation, so he simply states the truth as matter-of-factly as possible
jokes.explanation: A beautifully delivered understatement. The 'uh' mid-sentence sells the moment perfectly — George Michael almost lied, then gave up and told the truth, stated with the same banality as if he'd said 'I was just... sweeping.' The deadpan delivery of something terrible is the joke, and it's executed with great economy.

jokes.text: George Michael's desperate apology: 'I'll work weeknights. I'll lay people off. I'll give up my summer, all my summers. Just tell me what to do and I'll do it.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is apologizing to Michael for burning the banana stand
jokes.punchline: His over-compensating list of promises escalates absurdly — 'lay people off' (there's one employee), 'all my summers' — the severity doesn't match the situation
jokes.explanation: George Michael's panicked over-apology is funny through escalation — each promise reveals more about how he thinks business works. 'I'll lay people off' is especially funny since there's maybe one employee. The escalation to 'all my summers' is poignant and absurd simultaneously.

jokes.text: Michael's response to George Michael's distress: 'Burn it down.' / 'What?' / 'Let's burn this son of a bitch.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just watched his son despair about burning the banana stand; Michael is realizing he's done to George Michael what George Sr. did to him
jokes.punchline: Instead of consoling George Michael, Michael suggests burning it down himself — transforming the catastrophe into a bonding moment
jokes.explanation: A great punchline that works emotionally and comedically. 'Let's burn this son of a bitch' is unexpected, cathartic, and character-revealing — Michael, who has been the responsible one all episode, finally goes rogue. The line is genuinely funny and also a moment of genuine warmth. High quotability. Rewatch bonus because knowing the $250k is in the walls makes this devastating.

jokes.text: The three Bluth men — Michael, George Michael, and Gob — bonding over the burning banana stand
jokes.setup: Michael has decided to burn the stand down as a cathartic bonding moment
jokes.punchline: The visual of three grown men joyfully watching a building burn — presented as a warm family moment — is darkly funny; Gob has joined in on an activity he enjoys professionally
jokes.explanation: A sustained visual joke that's funnier the more you think about it. Gob's presence as the family arson enthusiast makes this particularly rich. The narrator's 'cathartic burning of the banana stand' gives it an ironic grandeur. Good character work across all three men.

jokes.text: Michael, George Michael, and Gob bond while burning the banana stand together — narrator: 'Michael, his son and his brother together enjoyed the cathartic burning of the banana stand.'
jokes.setup: The three Bluth men have decided to burn down the banana stand as a liberating act
jokes.punchline: The narrator describes this as 'cathartic' — treating the destruction of a business as a healthy emotional release — while the audience knows (post-reveal) that they've just burned $250,000
jokes.explanation: Good ironic narration. 'Cathartic burning' is a darkly funny descriptor for arson. The rewatch value is high — this scene looks very different when you know $250,000 is inside the walls.

jokes.text: 'You mailed that insurance check, right, Gob?'
jokes.setup: The banana stand is burning; Michael thinks back on logistics
jokes.punchline: The 'letter' Gob threw into the sea was the insurance check — the episode's entire arson plot now has a financial consequence that Michael has unknowingly signed off on
jokes.explanation: The episode's best structural joke and one of the great punchlines in Arrested Development history. Eight words that detonate everything. Michael's casual question — asked while the fire is already going — reveals he trusted Gob with the insurance check. We know Gob threw it in the sea. The silence or Gob's non-answer is the actual punchline. This joke requires the entire episode to set up (Gob's incompetence, the letter, the dramatic hurling, the banana stand's value) and pays off all of it simultaneously. Perfect economy, perfect structure, devastating character integration. This is the episode's peak comedy moment and would score favorably against any joke in television.

jokes.text: 'You mailed that insurance check, right, Gob?'
jokes.setup: The banana stand is burning; Michael and Gob have just shared a bonding moment
jokes.punchline: Michael asks if Gob mailed the insurance check — the audience already knows Gob hurled a letter (presumably this one) into the ocean. The question hangs unanswered and the stand burns.
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback construction. The question is perfectly timed — asked in the glow of a bonding moment as the banana stand burns — and the audience's knowledge that Gob threw the letter into the sea makes the unanswered question devastating. No answer is needed; the silence is the punchline. This is among the episode's best structural jokes and works beautifully on rewatch. Justifies the high craft score.

jokes.text: Narrator: Lindsay 'had slept through' her one day of employment, and Tobias 'couldn't hear the phone over the sound of the shower'
jokes.setup: Lindsay got a commercial job; Tobias is the runner-up
jokes.punchline: Lindsay sleeps through her call time; Tobias misses the callback because he's in the shower — both lose the job through pure incompetence unrelated to talent
jokes.explanation: A good structural joke that wraps up both the Lindsay and Tobias subplots. The irony that Tobias loses the job he desperately wanted not to a better actor but to his wife missing a call is good. The symmetry of both of them failing for logistical rather than talent reasons is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Tobias discovers Lindsay's conditioner improves his self-esteem; Lindsay compliments him: 'You look very handsome today.' / 'Thank you, Lindsay.'
jokes.setup: Michael had complained about Lindsay spending $68 on conditioner; Tobias uses it in the shower
jokes.punchline: The conditioner actually works — Lindsay sincerely compliments Tobias, and he accepts without knowing it's the conditioner — validating Lindsay's absurd expenditure while undercutting Michael's complaint
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback that pays off the conditioner subplot. The joke is that the conditioner genuinely works, which retroactively justifies Lindsay's purchase and undermines Michael's entire argument. Tobias's happy 'Thank you, Lindsay' without knowing why she's suddenly complimenting him is a sweet absurdist button.

jokes.text: Michael confronts George Sr.: 'Burned it right down to the ground.' / George Sr.: 'Are you crazy? There was money in that banana stand.'
jokes.setup: Michael visits his father to triumphantly report he burned the banana stand to show George Sr. who's boss
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s horror reveals what the audience now learns: there was literally cash — $250,000 — hidden in the walls of the banana stand
jokes.explanation: The episode's central revelation joke — the payoff of 'there's always money in the banana stand.' George Sr.'s cryptic advice, which seemed like folksy nonsense or mere sentiment, was completely literal. $250,000 in cash was in the walls. Michael's triumphant moment is revealed to be catastrophic. This is the structural punchline of the entire episode. The callback is perfect — every instance of 'there's always money in the banana stand' recontextualizes retroactively. Extraordinarily high structure, character integration, and earned quality. One of the best episode-level punchlines in the series.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'How much clearer can I say... There's always money in the banana stand?'
jokes.setup: Michael is devastated that he burned $250,000 in cash
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is exasperated that Michael didn't understand his obvious advice — 'there's always money in the banana stand' was perfectly clear — the joke is that from George Sr.'s perspective, he was being completely transparent
jokes.explanation: The triple-callback punchline. George Sr.'s genuine bewilderment that Michael didn't understand 'there's always money in the banana stand' as literal is the final twist — from George Sr.'s perspective, Michael is the idiot. The line becomes the show's most famous catchphrase and is delivered here with perfect exasperation. This is the joke that launched a thousand t-shirts and remains the show's most quoted line. High across all metrics.

jokes.text: 'No touching! No touching!' — both Michael and George Sr. through the prison glass
jokes.setup: Michael and George Sr. are having an emotional confrontation through prison glass
jokes.punchline: The guard's 'no touching' interruption — already a running gag — now fires in both directions: Michael and George Sr. are both reaching toward each other in distress, and the guard has to stop them both
jokes.explanation: A satisfying escalation of the 'no touching' running gag. The moment works because both characters are reaching for each other — one in despair ($250k gone), one in anger — and the guard's intervention is perfectly timed. The double 'No touching! No touching!' suggests both of them got called out. Good running gag payoff.

jokes.text: Tag: Gob protests the pet store's frozen dove exchange policy. 'Return from whence you came!'
jokes.setup: Gob has been trying to return a frozen dead dove to a pet store
jokes.punchline: He's apparently arguing with the pet store, and 'Return from whence you came!' suggests he's dramatically commanding the store to reverse its policy — or possibly addressing the dove
jokes.explanation: A great tag that pays off the dead dove running gag. 'Return from whence you came!' is wonderfully archaic and absurd for a pet store dispute. It also might be directed at the dove itself — commanding a dead frozen bird to go back to wherever it came from. High quotability, strong Gob character work. Good episode button.

jokes.text: Tag: Michael tells others the banana stand is being rebuilt; Gob complains: 'You couldn't have called me? I can't swing a hammer?'
jokes.setup: Michael is rebuilding the banana stand; Gob finds out after the fact
jokes.punchline: 'You couldn't have called me? I can't swing a hammer?' — Gob's grievance is about not being asked, directly echoing his 'I'd like to be asked' complaint from earlier
jokes.explanation: A perfect callback to the 'I'd like to be asked' joke. 'I can't swing a hammer?' directly parallels 'No! But I'd like to be asked' — Gob's complaint isn't about capability, it's about being included. The specificity of 'swing a hammer' (implying he might not actually be able to) adds another layer.

jokes.text: Tag final line: 'I got a rabbit to buy.' — Michael or George Michael on the banana stand reconstruction
jokes.setup: The banana stand is being rebuilt
jokes.punchline: 'I got a rabbit to buy' — a non-sequitur that implies a magic act rabbit is needed, possibly connecting to Gob's magic practice, possibly completely random
jokes.explanation: A mild absurdist tag. The line is puzzling enough to be funny but doesn't have the structural payoff of the other tag jokes. Possibly sets up something for a future episode. As a standalone line, it's a shrug — funny for being weird but not crafted enough to score higher.

jokes.text: 'I got a rabbit to buy.' — Gob's response/explanation
jokes.setup: Gob has just complained he wasn't called to help rebuild the banana stand
jokes.punchline: 'I got a rabbit to buy' — Gob's competing obligation is buying a rabbit (presumably for a magic trick), which undercuts his entire complaint about being excluded from work
jokes.explanation: The perfect button to the preview sequence. 'I got a rabbit to buy' perfectly undercuts Gob's complaint — he wants to be included in work but has a prior commitment to rabbit acquisition. The specificity of 'buy' rather than 'get' implies a whole transaction. Great economy.

tmdb_overview: Michael puts George Michael in charge of the Bluth Bannana Stand, and his son subsequently burns down the stand. Elsewhere, Tobias auditions for a commercial, and Lindsay inadvertantly gets the part.
insight: Deadpan understatement drives 88.8 score with 2.35 jokes per minute across 56 gags.
